caveats: a: The occupation source is broader than the six-digit industry.
a: Vertically integrated distribution can materially change the wage-weighted task mix.
rho: Failure costs are high because products are time-critical and emotionally sensitive.
rho: Some large suppliers already offer digital selection and ordering tools, reducing not-yet-automated opportunity.
e: Only five LMM firms are estimated in the frozen input, so a single classification error is material.
e: Large national manufacturers dominate visible public information.
s5: Establishments are not independent firms.
s5: Broad manufacturing transaction evidence does not distinguish going-concern sales from distressed asset exits.

q: Large-supplier capabilities may not reflect LMM pricing power.
d5: Disposition forecasts are not product-shipment data.
d5: A manufacturer that successfully shifts to cremation containers or adjacent memorial goods may outperform the current burial-casket basket.
o: Cremation substitution belongs principally in d5 rather than operator share.
o: Digital sales can change channel ownership without eliminating physical manufacture.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is primarily repeat business-to-business product supply to funeral professionals through catalogs, scheduled or rapid delivery, inventory programs, and personalized configurations, with some direct-to-consumer orders delivered to funeral homes under the Funeral Rule.
lens.excluded_activities: Concrete burial vault makers, funeral homes without casket manufacturing, pure distributors, urn-only or memorial-product firms, captive plants, and platform-scale casket companies outside the lower middle market.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of metal, wood, cloth-covered, oversize, veteran, personalized, and related burial caskets supplied repeatedly to independent funeral homes, distributors, cemeteries, and direct purchasers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover all miscellaneous manufacturing, not 339995. I map office, management, sales, design, order entry, personalization, scheduling, procurement, logistics planning, customer support, and quality-document work into partial AI exposure while leaving metal forming, welding, woodworking, finishing, upholstery, assembly, inspection, warehousing, and delivery largely physical. The broad group includes more technical medical manufacturing, so it may overstate casket exposure.
caveats: The occupation source is broader than the six-digit industry.
Vertically integrated distribution can materially change the wage-weighted task mix.
a.change_evidence: A 339995-specific occupational payroll mix and task-level study spanning production, personalization, order management, warehousing, and distribution would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The strongest AI opportunities sit in funeral-home order workflows, product selection, personalized art and configuration, demand and inventory planning, route scheduling, purchasing, and customer support. Physical fabrication, finishing, interior installation, quality assurance, and time-critical delivery remain labor-intensive.

d5.bridge: NFDA projects disposition shares rather than casket-manufacturer constant-quality demand, and CDC mortality counts measure deaths rather than burials. I combine a relatively stable annual death base with NFDA's projected 63.4% cremation versus 31.6% burial in 2025 and continuing cremation growth to infer a material five-year decline in burial-casket quantity. Cremation ceremonies using rental or combustible caskets may make the proxy overstate decline; green burial and no-casket choices may make it understate decline.
caveats: Disposition forecasts are not product-shipment data.
A manufacturer that successfully shifts to cremation containers or adjacent memorial goods may outperform the current burial-casket basket.
d5.change_evidence: Annual U.S. burial-casket unit shipments by material and use, combined with five-year disposition projections, rental-casket usage, and target-specific cremation-container exposure, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Mortality provides a durable need for disposition, but the relevant basket is burial caskets, not funeral activity generally. Cremation already exceeds burial by roughly two to one and is projected to keep gaining share, overwhelming modest demographic support for deaths.

caveats: Only five LMM firms are estimated in the frozen input, so a single classification error is material.
Large national manufacturers dominate visible public information.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level map of manufacturing status, customer recurrence, corporate affiliation, burial-casket revenue, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Casket manufacturers generally serve recurring external funeral-professional demand and fit the lens structurally. Ineligibility comes from captive or corporate facilities, direct-only models without repeat outsourced relationships, firms outside the EBITDA band, or businesses misclassified around distribution and adjacent memorial products.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 339995 ($168,384,001) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 339995 ($710,907,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $223,978 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 339995); class <5: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,956 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $818,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $160,544 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,197,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $431,150 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,874,778 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $956,431 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,316,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,182,356 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $127,877,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,089,467 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 5

caveats: Cremation substitution belongs principally in d5 rather than operator share.
Digital sales can change channel ownership without eliminating physical manufacture.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on direct-to-consumer penetration, funeral-home private labeling, local fabrication, rental reuse, alternative disposition, and digital selection that disintermediates current manufacturers would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Where burial-casket demand remains, customers need a fabricated, finished, inspected, and delivered physical product. AI can enable self-service selection, personalization, and ordering but cannot replace the casket itself with software.

q.bridge: Manufacturer pages document personalization, broad material and price-point choice, digital selection, rapid nationwide distribution, and next-scheduled delivery, while the FTC requires itemized casket prices and acceptance of outside caskets without surcharge. I infer retention from reliability and personalization but discount for transparent consumer alternatives, funeral-home procurement, and declining category volumes. The proxy may understate capture for locally entrenched rapid-delivery suppliers and overstate it for undifferentiated models.
caveats: FTC rules govern funeral providers, not manufacturer transfer prices directly.
Large-supplier capabilities may not reflect LMM pricing power.
q.change_evidence: Customer contract terms and gross-margin bridges after administrative or logistics productivity gains, segmented by standard, personalized, funeral-home, distributor, and direct orders, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Time-critical availability, finish quality, funeral-home relationships, and personalization support some margin retention. Price-list transparency, outside-casket rights, direct sellers, large national competitors, and shrinking burial share create substantial pass-through and price pressure.

caveats: Failure costs are high because products are time-critical and emotionally sensitive.
Some large suppliers already offer digital selection and ordering tools, reducing not-yet-automated opportunity.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots on order accuracy, personalization cycle time, customer-support handling, inventory turns, route planning, and administrative labor at comparable manufacturers would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Within five years, operators can deploy AI for catalog and specification search, order capture, personalization proofs, inventory forecasting, delivery routing, purchasing, document drafting, and funeral-home support. Small scale, legacy order systems, emotional sensitivity, exact delivery requirements, and physical quality checks constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell observes broad manufacturing transactions and Census owner-characteristics data are economy-wide; neither measures 339995 control transfers. I use them to infer a moderate five-year succession probability but reduce it for a 72-establishment niche, concentration among large suppliers, and the secular demand headwind that may produce closures or asset sales rather than qualifying transfers. Broad transaction data likely overstate viable control-sale probability.
caveats: Establishments are not independent firms.
Broad manufacturing transaction evidence does not distinguish going-concern sales from distressed asset exits.
s5.change_evidence: A full ownership map of casket manufacturers with owner age, corporate affiliation, financial health, closures, and recent strategic or control sales would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Independent family manufacturers can face succession, but a tiny target population and declining burial share narrow buyer appetite and increase the risk that an owner exit is not a transferable going concern.

principal_driver: Time-critical repeat orders and personalization create information and logistics workflows where AI can improve accuracy and speed around an indispensable physical product.
principal_weakness: Cremation's sustained share gains create a material five-year decline in the current burial-casket demand basket.
